column752: Attrition was limited and clearly reported, reasons for dropout were described, final analyzed sample sizes were balanced (Pilates n=25, control n=27).
column754: Outcomes were assessed using objective, computerized neuropsychological tests (CPT, WCST).
column756: No publicly available preregistered analysis plan or trial registry outcomes were referenced.
column758: No clear intention-to-treat analysis was described, and the passive control group inflate apparent intervention affects.
column760: The attrition and detection biases are low. Lack of binding, incomplete reporting on randomization procedure, and passive control group prevent classification as low risk.
column762: It enrolled a highly selected population: adult females only, ages 20–50, required a confirmed ADHD diagnosis, minimum IQ threshold, no regular physical activity, no recent CBT or Pilates, exclusion of other psychiatric disorders, and exclusion of participants using stimulant medication during the study.
column763: Highly selected intervention team or level of training/proficiency not widely available
column764: The Pilates intervention was delivered by a trained physical coach with ≥5 years of Pilates experience, following a structured, progressive protocol over 24 weeks, with high supervision and adherence monitoring.
column765: Inadequate comparison therapy or use of a substandard alternative therapy
column766: The control group received no intervention (passive control).
column768: The study’s outcomes are objective neuropsychological test measures (Continuous Performance Task and Wisconsin Card Sorting Test).
column769: Level of care different from that in the community
column770: It was conducted in specialty ADHD centers with laboratory-based cognitive testing and a highly structured, supervised Pilates program delivered by an experienced instructor.
column773: There is no indication that participants were systematically asked about harms during or after the intervention.
column775: Adverse events are not reported, and the study does not specify the number of participants experiencing adverse events.

column34: Adults receiving care in a specialist adult ADHD clinic with a confirmed clinical ADHD diagnosis, most diagnosed since 2020, most taking prescribed ADHD medication, major psychiatric and neurodevelopmental comorbidities systematically excluded
column153: All participants were adults already under care in an NHS Adult ADHD service, and eligibility required age ≥18 years with a confirmed ADHD diagnosis documented in their clinical record
column253: Coaching, ADAPT (Autonomy, Design, Awareness, Psychoeducation, and Training Integration for Sustainable Change) framework plus usual ADHD care consisting of one 2-hour online assessment session exploring personal challenges and lived experience of ADHD including foundational neuroaffirmative self-determination theory-based psychoeducation focused on neurobiological responses to environmental engagement, followed by ten 1-hour online individually focused therapeutic coaching sessions emphasizing autonomy-supportive client-led problem identification, development of self-awareness of motivational factors, context-oriented strategy development, and support for neurobiological and basic psychological needs, delivered over an average of 11 sessions, for 15 weeks
column259: Waitlist control continuing usual ADHD care
column397: Symptoms of functional impairment were reduced in the intervention group compared to the waitlist control at posttreatment.
column427: ADHDRS-IV-Inv (Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder Rating Scale, Investigator-Administered)
column453: ADHD symptom severity decreased from baseline to posttreatment in both groups, with larger reductions in the intervention group than in the waitlist control .
column623:
SR&I (Self-Regulation and Integration Scale) Self-regulation improved more in the intervention group than in the waitlist control at posttreatment (p<0.01).
CORE-OM (Clinical Outcomes in Routine Evaluation–Outcome Measure) Quality of life improved more in the intervention group than in the waitlist control, although between-group effects were not statistically significant.
PQ (Personal Questionnaire) Patient-identified goal progress improved more in the intervention group than in the waitlist control.
column711: 11/12 intervention participants completed the program, with approximately 11 sessions completed over 15 weeks
column748: Baseline characteristics appear broadly comparable, but the reporting is not detailed enough to rule out selection bias with certainty.
column750: Participants and clinicians could not be blinded because the intervention was a psychosocial coaching program versus waitlist control, and awareness of allocation may have influenced participant engagement and self-reported outcomes.
column754: Most outcomes were self-report or clinician-rated, and blinding of outcome assessors was not reported and unlikely in a behavioral waitlist-controlled trial.
column756: Key prespecified outcomes were reported, but as this was a feasibility trial without a clear pre-published statistical analysis plan, selective outcome emphasis cannot be ruled out.
column758: Small feasibility sample size increases instability of estimates, but this is expected for early-phase work.
column760: Because participants and assessors were not blinded and outcomes were subjective (symptoms, functioning, QoL), there is a substantial risk that awareness of allocation influenced outcomes, even though attrition was low and randomization appeared acceptable.
column762: Participants were adults with clinically confirmed ADHD in a specialist NHS service, but eligibility criteria excluded multiple psychiatric and neurodevelopmental comorbidities.
column764: The ADAPT program is a structured psychosocial therapeutic coaching intervention delivered online by clinicians over approximately 15 weeks, and reflects a realistic intensity and format for ADHD psychosocial treatment in clinical practice.
column766: Waitlist control with continuation of usual NHS ADHD care represents a realistic and interpretable comparator for assessing the added value of a psychosocial intervention.
column768: Outcomes included ADHD symptoms, functional impairment, quality of life, and mental health functioning assessed at approximately 15 weeks, all of which are clinically relevant ADHD treatment outcomes.
column770: The intervention was delivered in a standard NHS specialist outpatient ADHD service using an online format consistent with routine clinical practice.
column773: There was no formal adverse-event monitoring system described.
column775: The study did not systematically report adverse events or serious adverse events, and the number of participants experiencing AEs was not reported.
